subject: Mathematics
q: The proposition $\sim(p \vee \sim q) \vee \sim(p \vee q)$ is logically equivalent to:
question: The proposition $\sim(p \vee \sim q) \vee \sim(p \vee q)$ is logically equivalent to:
solution:
Given $\sim(p \vee \sim q) \vee \sim(p \vee q)$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\equiv(\sim p \vee q) \vee(\sim p \vee \sim q) \\
&\equiv \sim p \vee(q \vee \sim q) \\
&\equiv \sim p
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: The negation of the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>∧</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equivalent to
question: The negation of the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>∧</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equivalent to
solution:
The negation of q∨((~q)∧p) is

~(q∨((~q)∧p))

≡~q∧~((~q)∧p)

≡~q∧(q∨~p)

≡(~q∧q)∨(~q∧~p)

≡(~q∧~p)

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following Boolean expressions is not a tautology?
question: Which of the following Boolean expressions is not a tautology?
solution:
1 (p⇒q)∨(~q⇒p)

By applying a⇒b≡~a∨b & ~~a=a, we get

=(~p∨q)∨(q∨p)

Since, the operation disjunction is commutative, hence, we have

=(~p∨q)∨(p∨q)

Now, applying the distributive law i.e. a∨b∨c≡a∨b∨a∨c, we get

=(~p∨p)∨q

Also, we know that ~a∨a≡t & t∨a≡t, where t represents tautology.

Thus, we have

=t∨q=t.

Hence, the option A is a tautology.

2 (q⇒p)∨(~q⇒p)

Again, using the same definitions, we get

=(~q∨p)∨(q∨p)

=(~q∨q)∨p

=t∨p=t.

Hence, the option B is also a tautology.

3 (p⇒~q)∨(~q⇒p)

=(~p∨~q)∨(q∨p)

=(~p∨p)∨(~q∨q)

=t∨t=t.

Hence, the option C is also a tautology.

4 (~p⇒q)∨(~q⇒p)

=(p∨q)∨(q∨p)

=(p∨q)∨(p∨q)

=p∨q.

Which is not a tautology.

q: The Boolean expression (p∧q)⇒r∧q∧p is equivalent to:
question: The Boolean expression (p∧q)⇒r∧q∧p is equivalent to:
solution:
(p∧q)⇒((r∧q)∧p)≡(p∧q)⇒(r∧(p∧q))…….(i)

Case: I when p∧q=T and r=F

Statement-(i) is false as well as option (3) is also false
option (1), option (2) are True so given expression is not equivalent to option (1), option (2)

option (4) may be true if we consider q=T∴ option (4) is also not equivalent to option (1) Case : II p∧q=False

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following is equivalent to the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>~</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo> </mo><mo>?</mo></math>
question: Which of the following is equivalent to the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>~</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo> </mo><mo>?</mo></math>
solution:
Which of the following is equivalent to the Boolean expression p∧~q ?

For option 1

~p→~q≡p∨~q

≢p∧~q 

For Option 2

~q→p≡~~q∨p≡q∧~p

≢p∧~q

For option 3

~(p→q)

≡~(~p∨q)

≡(p∧~q)

For option 4

~p→~q≡~~p∨~q≡p∧q

≢p∧~q

subject: Mathematics
q: The negation of the statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>B</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>)</mo><mo>⇒</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>B</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>)</mo><mo>⇒</mo><mi>A</mi></math> is
question: The negation of the statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>B</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>)</mo><mo>⇒</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>B</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>)</mo><mo>⇒</mo><mi>A</mi></math> is
solution:
Given,

((A∧(B∨C))⇒(A∨B))⇒A

We know that, X⇒Y≡~X∨Y,

Now using the above formula we get,

≡~(A∧(B∨C))∨(A∨B)⇒A

≡~(~(A∧(B∨C))∨(A∨B))∨A

≡(A∧(B∨C))∧~(A∨B)∨A

Now to solve (A∧(B∨C))∧~(A∨B) let us assume a universal set 1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8, set A=1,2,3, set B=3,4,5 and set C=5,6,7

So, A∧B∨C will be 3 and ~(A∨B) will be 6,7,8, 

So, (A∧(B∨C))∧~(A∨B)=∅

≡F∨A

≡A

∴ Negation of statement=~A

subject: Mathematics
q: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is
question: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Using logical equivalence condition p→q≡~p∨q, given statement can be expressed as, 

p∧~q→p→~q

≡ ~p∧~q∨~p∨~q.......(1)

We know De Morgan's law of logical equivalence is,

~p∧q≡~p∨~q

Applying De Morgan's law in equation(1) we get,

≡ ~p∨q∨~p∨~q   ∵~~p≡p

≡ ~p∨~p∨q∨~q

≡~p∨t≡t (tautology)

q: The compound statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>~</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>)</mo><mo>∨</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>∧</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>⇒</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>)</mo></math> is equivalent to
question: The compound statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>~</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>)</mo><mo>∨</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>∧</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>⇒</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>)</mo></math> is equivalent to
solution:
We have,

P Q ~P∧Q ~P∧Q ~P∧Q∨~P∧Q ~P∧~Q ~P∧Q∨~P∧Q⇒~P∧~Q
T T F F F F T
T F T F T F F
F T T T T F F
F F T F T T T

Also,

P Q ~P ~Q ~P∨Q ~Q∨P ~P∧Q∧~Q∨P
T T F F T T T
T F F T T F F
F T T F F F F
F F T T T T T

Hence, ~P∧Q∨~P∧Q⇒~P∧~Q≡~P∧Q∧~Q∨P.

q: If the truth value of the statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is false <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>F</mi></mfenced></math>, then the truth values of the statements <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>r</mi></math> are respectively
question: If the truth value of the statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is false <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>F</mi></mfenced></math>, then the truth values of the statements <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>r</mi></math> are respectively
solution:
Recall, p→q is false if and only if p is true and q is false

Alternatively,

Truth table of statement p→~q∨r

p q r ~q ~q∨r p→~q∨r
T T T F T T
T T F F F F
T F T T T T
T F F T T T
F T T F T T
F T F F F T
F F T T T T
F F F T T T

So, the truth values are p: true, q: true, r: false

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is false, then the truth values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> are, respectively
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is false, then the truth values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> are, respectively
solution:
p→q has truth value false only when p is true & q is false. Hence, truth value of p is true.

Now, ~p∨~q is false only when ~p & ~q both are false.

Hence, p and q are both true.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Contrapositive of the statement :

'If a function f is differentiable at a, then it is also continuous at a', is
question:
Contrapositive of the statement :

'If a function f is differentiable at a, then it is also continuous at a', is
options: If a function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is continuous at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>, then it is not differentiable at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>.

If a function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is not continuous at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>, then it is not differentiable at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>.

If a function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is not continuous at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>. then it is differentiable at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>.

If a function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is continuous at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>, then it is differentiable at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>.
solution:
By using definition, we have;

Contrapositive of p⇒q is ~q⇒~p.

Which means contrapositive of given statement is;

"If a function f is not continuous at a, then it is not differentiable at a."

subject: Mathematics
q: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>~</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to
question: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>~</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to
solution:
Given,

~p∨~p∧q

Now we know that,

By demorgan law, ~A∨B≡~A∧~B

Now using above formula we get,

~p∨~p∧q

≡~p∧p∧q as ~~A≡A

≡p∧q∧~p

subject: Mathematics
q: The contrapositive of the following statement, "If the side of a square doubles, then its area increases four times", is
question: The contrapositive of the following statement, "If the side of a square doubles, then its area increases four times", is
options: if the area of a square increases four times, then its side is not doubled.
if the area of a square increases four times, then its side is doubled.
if the area of a square does not increase four times, then its side is not doubled.
if the side of a square is not doubled, then its area does not increase four times.
solution:
Contrapositive of p→q is given by ~q→~p.

So, contrapositive of given statement is, "If the area of a square does not increase four times, then its side is not doubled".

subject: Mathematics
q: The logical statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to
question: The logical statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to
solution:
~~p∨q∨p∧r∧~q∧r

=p∧~q∨p∧r∧~q∧r

By distributive law

=p∧~q∨r∧~q∧r

p∧~q∧r≡p∧r∧~q

=p∧∼q∧r

By Associative law

=p∧r∧~q

subject: Mathematics
q: Which one of the following statements is not a tautology?
question: Which one of the following statements is not a tautology?
solution:
Logical Statement p→q is false if and only if p is true and q is false.

So, when p is false, q is true, p∨q→p∨~q will be false.

Clearly p∨q→p∨~q is not a tautology.

subject: Mathematics
q: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to
question: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to
solution:
Given,

p∧q⇒p∧r

{Using A⇒B≡~A∨B} 

=~p∧q∨p∧r

{Using Demorgan's law ~A∧B≡~A∨~B}

=~p∨~q∨p∧r

=~p∨p∧r∨~q

=~p∨p∧~p∨r∨~q

=~p∨r∨~q

=~p∨~q∨r

=~p∧q∨r

=p∧q⇒r

q: If the truth value of the statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>P</mi><mo>∧</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mi>Q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>, then the truth value of which of the following is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>?
question: If the truth value of the statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>P</mi><mo>∧</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mi>Q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>, then the truth value of which of the following is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>?
solution:
We know that A→B is false, when A is true and B is false

So, for P∧~R→~R∧Q to be false, P∧~R should be true and ~R∧Q is false.

i.e P is true, R is false and Q is false.

A B A→B
T T T
T F F
F T T
F F T

Now, 

for P∨Q→~R is F→T≡T

R∨Q→~P is F→F≡T

~P∨Q→~R is T→T≡T

~R∨Q→~P is T→F≡F

solution:
Let P=A⊆B,Q=B⊆D,R=A⊆C

Contrapositive of P∧Q→R is

~R→~P∧Q=~R→~P∨~Q

subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider the following statements:

P: Ramu is intelligent.

Q: Ramu is rich.

R: Ramu is not honest.

The negation of the statement "Ramu is intelligent and honest if and only if Ramu is not rich" can be expressed as:
question:
Consider the following statements:

P: Ramu is intelligent.

Q: Ramu is rich.

R: Ramu is not honest.

The negation of the statement "Ramu is intelligent and honest if and only if Ramu is not rich" can be expressed as:
solution:
Given,

P: Ramu is intelligent

Q: Ramu is rich

R: Ramu is not honest

Given statement, "Ramu is intelligent and honest if and only if Ramu is not rich"

=P∧~R⇔~Q

So, negation of the statement is ~P∧~R⇔~Q

=~~P∧~R∨~Q∧Q∨P∧~R

[using A⇔B=~A∨B∧~B∨A]

=P∧~R∧Q∨~Q∧~P∨R

[by using~A∨B=~A∧~B and ~A∧B=~A∨~B]

subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider the following two statements:

P : If 7 is an odd number, then 7 is divisible by 2.

Q : If 7 is a prime number, then 7 is an odd number.

If V1 is the truth value of the contrapositive of P and V2 is the truth value of contrapositive of Q, then the ordered pair V1,V2 equals
question:
Consider the following two statements:

P : If 7 is an odd number, then 7 is divisible by 2.

Q : If 7 is a prime number, then 7 is an odd number.

If V1 is the truth value of the contrapositive of P and V2 is the truth value of contrapositive of Q, then the ordered pair V1,V2 equals
solution:
Contrapositive of P:

7 is not divisible by 2⇒7 is not odd number

T ⇒F:FV1

Contrapositive of Q:

7 is not odd number ⇒7 is not a prime number  

F⇒ F:T V2

Thus, V1,V2≡F,T

solution:
Given ∽∽s∨∽r∧s=s∧∽∽r∧s

∵~p∧q=∽p∨∽q

and ∽p∨q=∽p∧∽q

=s∧r∨∽s

=s∧r∨s∧∽s

=s∧r∨F

=s∧r

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following statements is a tautology?
question: Which of the following statements is a tautology?
solution:
We know,

A→B≡~A∨B

Now solving all options one by one we get,

A p→p∧p→q

≡~p∨p∧~p∨q

≡~p∨f∨p∧q

≡~p ∨p∧q≡~p∨p∧~p∨q

≡~p ∨ q

Which is not a tautology

B p∧q→~p→q

≡~p∧q∨p∨q

≡~p∨~q∨p∨q

≡~p∨p∨~q∨q

≡T∨T≡T

Tautology

C p∧p→q→~q

≡~p∧~p∨q∨~q

≡~p∧q∨~q

≡~p∨~q

Which is not a tautology

D p∨p∧q≡p

Which is not a tautology

subject: Mathematics
q: Contrapositive of the statement "If two numbers are not equal, then their squares are not equal". is :
question: Contrapositive of the statement "If two numbers are not equal, then their squares are not equal". is :
options: If the squares of two numbers are not equal, then the numbers are equal.
If the squares of two numbers are equal, then the numbers are not equal.
If the squares of two numbers are equal, then the numbers are equal.
If the squares of two numbers are not equal, then the numbers are not equal.
solution: Contrapositive of "If $A$ then $B^{\prime \prime}$ is "If $\sim \mathrm{B}$ then $\sim A^{\prime \prime}$. Hence contrapositive of "If two numbers are not equal, then their squares are not equal" is "If squares of two numbers are equal, then the two numbers are equal".

q: The converse of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mi>r</mi></math> is
question: The converse of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mi>r</mi></math> is
solution:
We know that,

Converse of A⇒B is B⇒A

So, using the above formula we get,

Converse of ~p∧q⇒r 

≡r⇒(~p∧q)

≡~r∨~p∧q

≡~p∧q∨~r

≡~p∨~q∨~r as ~A∨B≡~A∧~B

≡p∨~q⇒~r

q: Which of the following is the negation of the statement "for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> there exists <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>S</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><msup><mi>M</mi><mrow><mo>'</mo><mo>'</mo></mrow></msup><mo>?</mo></math>
question: Which of the following is the negation of the statement "for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> there exists <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>S</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><msup><mi>M</mi><mrow><mo>'</mo><mo>'</mo></mrow></msup><mo>?</mo></math>
solution:
P: for all M>0, there exists x∈S such that x≥M

~P: there exists M>0, for all x∈S such that x<m 

Negation of 'there exsits' is 'for all'.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}, \mathrm{r}$ denote arbitrary statements. Then the logically equivalent of the statement $\mathrm{p} \Rightarrow(\mathrm{q} \vee \mathrm{r})$ is:
question: Let $\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}, \mathrm{r}$ denote arbitrary statements. Then the logically equivalent of the statement $\mathrm{p} \Rightarrow(\mathrm{q} \vee \mathrm{r})$ is:
solution:
Given statement is
$p \Rightarrow(q \vee r)$ which is equivalent to
$$
(p \Rightarrow q) \vee(p \Rightarrow r)
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following statements is a tautology?
question: Which of the following statements is a tautology?
solution:
Plotting the truth table of the following option we have,

p q ~p ~q ~p∨q
T T F F T
T F F T F
F T T F T
F F T T T

We know that, A⇒B is only false when A is true and B is false, now using this rule and above table we will make truth table of options,

~p∨q⇒p p⇒~p∨q ~p∨q⇒q q⇒~p∨q
T T T T
T F T T
F T T T
F T F T

 

Hence q⇒~p∨q is tautology.

subject: Mathematics
q: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to :
question: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to :
solution:
A→B→A

≡A→~B∨A

≡~A∨~B∨A

≡~A∨A∨~B

≡T∨~B≡T

∴ T∨B=T

≡~A∨A∨B

≡~A∨A∨B

≡A→A∨B

q: Let $p$ and $q$ be two Statements. Amongst the following, the Statement that is equivalent to $p \rightarrow q$ is
question: Let $p$ and $q$ be two Statements. Amongst the following, the Statement that is equivalent to $p \rightarrow q$ is
solution: Let $p$ and $q$ be two statements. $p \rightarrow q$ is equivalent to $\sim p \vee q$.
